title: Universal Spin Squeezing Dynamical Phase Transitions across Lattice Geometries, Dimensions, and Microscopic Couplings
abstract: Recent work has identified a dynamical squeezing phase transition in power-law interacting bilayer XXZ spin models, separating a fully collective phase with Heisenberg-limited squeezing from a partially-collective phase with universal critical scaling. Here we test and establish the universality of this transition along two qualitatively different microscopic axes: lattice geometry, by studying square, triangular, and honeycomb $2\mathrm{D}$ bilayers as well as $1\mathrm{D}$ ladders, and a symmetry-preserving rescaling $λ$ of the interlayer couplings relative to the intralayer ones. Combining a Bogoliubov instability analysis with discrete truncated Wigner simulations, we find that the transition persists across all four lattice geometries and over a wide range of $λ$ with critical exponents consistent within error, providing strong evidence for a genuine non-equilibrium universality class. The Bogoliubov theory recovers the previously identified scaling $a_Z^* \propto L$ in the long-range interacting regime $α< d+2$, and yields an analytical scaling $a_Z^* \propto L^{2/(α-d)}$ for the critical aspect ratio with system size for $α>d+2$, with $α$ the power-law exponent in dimension $d$. This uncovers a previously unrecognized sub-linear regime for short-range interactions. By tuning $λ$ we vary the interlayer coupling strength at fixed layer spacing, demonstrating that the dynamical transition can be driven purely through interaction engineering without modifying the underlying geometry. These findings provide a versatile route toward controlling entanglement generation in Rydberg-array, polar molecule, and trapped-ion platforms with applications in quantum sensing and simulation.
summary: This paper proves that the dynamical phase transition in spin squeezing is universal across different lattice geometries and dimensions. It identifies a new scaling regime for short-range interactions and shows that the transition can be controlled by tuning interlayer couplings, offering a way to engineer entanglement in quantum simulators.
main_problem: The study investigates the universality of a dynamical spin-squeezing phase transition in power-law interacting bilayer spin models across different lattice geometries, dimensions, and coupling strengths.
main_result: The authors establish that the dynamical squeezing transition is universal across various 1D and 2D geometries and identify a new sub-linear scaling regime for the critical aspect ratio in the short-range interaction regime.
method: The research combines Bogoliubov instability analysis using Holstein-Primakoff transformations with numerical simulations using the discrete Truncated Wigner Approximation (dTWA).
model_or_system: The system consists of power-law interacting spin-1/2 bilayer XXZ-type models, including 1D ladders and 2D square, triangular, and honeycomb lattices, with interactions decaying as r^-alpha.
key_observables: Spin squeezing (variance of mixed quadratures), phase sensitivity, and the scaling exponent p of the minimal variance.
important_parameters: Power-law exponent (alpha), interlayer coupling ratio (lambda), and the aspect ratio (aZ/L).
main_assumptions: The Bogoliubov analysis is a quadratic-order approximation that may systematically underestimate the critical aspect ratio due to the omission of non-linear corrections.
figures_summary: Figures compare Bogoliubov and dTWA predictions for phase boundaries, show scaling collapses for different lattice geometries and coupling ratios, and illustrate momentum-dependent dispersion relations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the dynamical transition, presents Bogoliubov-based analytical scaling laws, demonstrates universality via numerical scaling collapses across geometries and couplings, and discusses experimental implications for Rydberg and trapped-ion platforms.
why_interesting: It provides a versatile route for controlling entanglement generation through interaction engineering and uncovers new scaling physics in the short-range regime, which is highly relevant for designing quantum sensors in Rydberg and trapped-ion arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Comparison of phase-boundary within Bogoliubov (dashed) analysis and dTWA (solid). Critical aspect ratio (a∗ Z/L) (Bogoliubov, dashed line) as a function of power-law exponent α for 1D and different 2D lattice structures compared with dTWA results (solid line).

FIG. 2. Comparison of phase-boundary within Bogoliubov (dashed) analysis and dTWA (solid). Critical aspect ratio (a∗ Z/L) (Bogoliubov, dashed line) as a function of the ratio between interlayer and intralayer interactions λ separating the region, in which only the k = 0 mode is unstable (shaded blue), and the region with multiple unstable modes compared with dTWA results (solid line).

FIG. 3. System size dependence of critical aspect ratio (a∗ Z/L) for different power-law exponents α. (a) a∗ Z/L as a function of α for different system size L. For α < d + 2 (light blue) a∗ Z/L is independent of L, whereas for α ≥d + 2 it decreases with system size. (b) a∗ Z/L as a function of L for different α. Dashed lines are the analytical predictions (see text).
